moveAnnotations.annotation: Welcome to the Polish Opening, a rare but principled first move that immediately challenges Black's setup. We're staking a claim on the queenside by controlling c5 and a5, while keeping our position flexible—we haven't committed our center yet. This move was favored by Sokolsky and other creative players who preferred off-beat positions to well-trodden theory. By playing b4, we're signaling that we want to play on the wings rather than fight for e4, and we're hoping Black will either mirror us or occupy the center, where we can counterattack with moves like e4 or c4 later.

Black mirrors our strategy with perfect symmetry, which is why this variation is called Symmetrical. By playing b5, Black controls a4 and c5, just as we control c5 and a5—but now both sides have committed the b-pawn and neither has a clear advantage. This symmetry actually slightly favors White, because we move first and can break the symmetry when it suits us (perhaps with e4, c4, or even a4). The position now requires patience and understanding of imbalanced pawn structures—we'll likely strive for a move that gives us the first real advantage while Black has few clear options to equalize.
